title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read four extracts from internet articles about technology in sports. For questions 37 – 40, choose from the experts A – D. The expert may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Synthetic Muse: AI and Art

texts.A: The emergence of AI-generated imagery is being hailed as the 'democratization of art,' but this is a shallow interpretation. True art is an act of communication between two conscious minds, rooted in lived experience. [37]While some claim that AI is just another tool like the camera, I believe it is fundamentally different because it lacks the 'intent' that defines a human creator.[/37] [39]Furthermore, the way these models are trained—by scraping the work of millions of living artists without consent—is a form of digital colonialism that will eventually bankrupt the creative class.[/39] We are not expanding the boundaries of art; we are automating the soul out of it.
texts.B: We are currently in a period of 'techno-panic' that mirrors the arrival of photography in the 19th century. Back then, critics claimed the camera would kill painting; instead, it forced painting to become more abstract and expressive. [40]AI will do the same, taking over the labor-intensive tasks of rendering and shading, allowing artists to focus on high-level conceptual work.[/40] [38]I disagree with those who see AI as a threat to human unique creativity; it is a collaborative partner that can suggest visual combinations a human brain would never conceive.[/38] [37]I share the view that the 'artist' is simply moving from the brush to the prompt, and the resulting work is no less valid for its medium.[/37]
texts.C: The ethical debate over AI art often ignores the fact that human artists have always 'borrowed' from their predecessors. No one creates in a vacuum. However, the scale and speed of AI mimicry are unprecedented. [39]I share the concern that the legal system is woefully unprepared to deal with the copyright implications of generative models, which could lead to a world where human artists cannot compete with their own algorithmic shadows.[/39] [40]That said, I am skeptical that AI will lead to an 'evolution' in art; more likely, it will lead to a glut of hyper-realistic, aesthetically pleasing but emotionally vacant content that devalues the very idea of craftsmanship.[/40] The machine can replicate the style, but never the struggle.
texts.D: The power of AI lies in its ability to act as a mirror to our collective unconscious. By analyzing millions of images, it reveals the patterns and biases of our visual culture. [38]I am less convinced than some of my colleagues that this technology is a 'partner' in any meaningful sense; it is a sophisticated mirror, nothing more.[/38] [37]However, I disagree with the pessimistic view that AI-generated work lacks 'intent'; the human who writes the prompt, curates the output, and refines the result is exercising a clear, albeit new, form of artistic agency.[/37] The real danger is not the machine, but the commercial pressure to prioritize 'algorithmic' art that satisfies a data-driven market over the idiosyncratic visions of individuals.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether the person using AI can be considered a legitimate artist with 'intent'?
questions.explanation: A argues that AI 'lacks the intent that defines a human creator.' B, however, argues that the 'artist' is simply moving from the brush to the prompt, validating their role.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a different opinion to B regarding AI's role as a 'collaborative partner' in the creative process?
questions.explanation: B calls AI a 'collaborative partner' that conceives new combinations. D is 'less convinced' it's a partner, describing it instead as a 'sophisticated mirror, nothing more.'

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the impact of AI on the livelihood or legal rights of human artists?
questions.explanation: A calls the training of AI 'digital colonialism' that will 'bankrupt the creative class.' C shares this concern, noting the 'copyright implications' and that artists might not be able to compete with 'algorithmic shadows.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the long-term effect of AI on the quality or evolution of art?
questions.explanation: B believes AI will allow artists to focus on 'high-level conceptual work' (an evolution). C is 'skeptical' of this, predicting instead a 'glut' of 'emotionally vacant' content that 'devalues the idea of craftsmanship.'
